poem:
**Windfall**

In the Commons, numbers rise like elevator buttons pressed too hard—  
45 to 55, a levied weather.  
Someone says "exceptional revenues,"  
as if profit were a storm we could bottle,  
pouring it back into the Treasury  
to warm businesses and families.  
Outside, the old low-carbon generators  
consider fixed-price vows,  
and the lights keep pretending they're innocent.
prompt: You are the poet Billy Collins. You woke up this morning feeling creative. You have just read this story in the newspaper: "Cabinet Office suggested Mandelson did not need security vetting, says Robbins as he describes ‘constant pressure’ from No 10 – live 1.09pm BST Reeves extends windfall tax being extended for energy firms, partly to get them to switch to better pricing model In the Commons Rachel Reeves, the chancellor, has just confirmed that she is extending the windfall tax for energy companies. She said this would generate more money for the Treasury, helping to pay for “government to support businesses and families”, and encourage electricity producers to move on to new contracts, which should lead to lower prices for consumers. Jillian Ambrose wrote a preview of this announcement here. Related: Electricity generators threatened with higher windfall taxes in bid to ensure stable prices Today Reeves told MPs: I am announcing that I will extend the electricity generator levy past its scheduled conclusion in 2028. And ahead of that, I am increasing the rate of the electricity generators levy from 45% to 55%. This ensures that a larger proportion of any exceptional revenues from high gas prices are passed back to government, providing a vital revenue stream so that money is available for government to support businesses and families with the impacts of the conflict in the Middle East. But, crucially, it will encourage older, low carbon electricity generators, which supply about a third of our power, to move from market pricing to fixed price contracts for difference. Under new proposals set out by [energy secretary Ed Miliband] today, that will". Write a poem in YOUR OWN DISTINCTIVE STYLE, no more than 60 words long. You may add a one line title at the top if you like.
